reasoning.typography: Sohne is a strong, distinctive choice with excellent weight distribution. Code snippets in Source Code Pro show discipline. Not perfect—hero text could be tighter-leaded.
reasoning.hierarchy: Hero dominates appropriately, but competing CTAs ('Get started' + Google sign-up) dilute focus. Lower sections flatten into sameness—headlines don't do enough visual work.
reasoning.distinctiveness: Gradient blobs are Stripe's signature now, but the 'Accept payments globally' + 'Enable billing' structure is pure fintech stencil. Swap for Adyen and it'd work fine.
reasoning.color: Branded gradient (blue-pink-orange) is controlled but occupies ~40% of hero viewport—not accent territory. Below-fold reverts to safe blue/purple. Disciplined but not restrained.
reasoning.density: Confident whitespace in hero. Logo parade breathes properly. Product cards below show good padding discipline without generic uniformity. Actual content density, not fear-of-space.
reasoning.voice: Opens strong ('your billionth') but IA defaults to 'Solutions for every business model' → feature grid. Classic product homepage structure—doesn't reflect Stripe's actual complexity/story.

winning_patterns: Sohne typeface creates immediate brand recognition—not Inter/default sans
Hero gradient is genuinely ownable visual territory at this point
Confident scale contrast between stat ticker and headline
Logo parade has proper weight hierarchy (not all same size)
Product screenshots show actual UI, not decorated mockups
anti_patterns: Dual CTAs in hero split attention—pick one dominant action
'Flexible solutions for every business model' is generic B2B speak
Below-fold structure is paint-by-numbers: feature 1, feature 2, testimonials incoming
Gradient occupies too much viewport %—decorative not functional
Gray subheads lack personality—could be any fintech's microcopy
